frameworks.name: Bayesian causal inference
frameworks.one_line: brain infers common vs separate causes of multisensory cues to guide integration

frameworks.name: hierarchical multisensory processing
frameworks.one_line: fused estimate computed first, then filtered by inferred causal structure

frameworks.name: forced sensory fusion
frameworks.one_line: obligatory cue combination irrespective of common source

frameworks.name: prefrontal adaptive behaviour control
frameworks.one_line: frontal cortex selects behaviour from candidate sensory representations

core_question: When and where in the human brain does multisensory causal inference emerge along the processing hierarchy?
key_assumptions: multisensory perception requires deciding whether cues share an origin
computations can be localised in time and source from MEG
fused and segregated estimates coexist as candidate representations
manipulations: multisensory cues varying in their inferred common vs separate cause
time-resolved and source-resolved MEG analysis stages
key_findings: early segregated unisensory representations precede integration
sensory fusion arises in parietal-temporal regions
causal inference computations culminate in frontal lobe
systematic spatio-temporal cascade across the hierarchy
interpretations: prefrontal cortex guides flexible integrative behaviour from sensory/association candidates
multisensory integration is an instance of generalised adaptive behaviour
